question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized + take (taker authorization)] Can an authorized operator call Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, units, maker, maker, address(0), hex'') with taker=maker to force maker into a debt position (as seller) without maker's direct consent, violating the invariant that debt increases must require the borrower's explicit action or authorization? Proof idea: unit test; assert that take with taker=maker by an authorized operator increases maker's debt and confirm whether this is blocked or permitted by the authorization model.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultSettlementFee + touchMarket] Can the feeSetter call setDefaultSettlementFee(loanToken, index, maxFee) before a market is created, causing all subsequently created markets for that loanToken to inherit the maximum settlement fee, and can a market creator exploit this by creating a market immediately after the feeSetter sets a zero default fee to lock in zero fees permanently? Proof idea: state-transition test; assert that defaultSettlementFeeCbp[loanToken] at market creation time is correctly snapshotted into marketState and cannot be retroactively changed by setDefaultSettlementFee.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultContinuousFee + touchMarket] Can the feeSetter set defaultContinuousFee[loanToken]=MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE before market creation, causing all new markets for that loanToken to inherit the maximum continuous fee, and does this create a DoS where lenders' pendingFee grows so fast that their effective credit approaches zero before maturity? Proof idea: fuzz (continuousFee, timeToMaturity, creditAmount) and assert pendingFee <= credit at all times.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee (index boundary)] Can the feeSetter call setMarketSettlementFee(id, 7, fee) with index=7 (out of range) and does the require(index <= 6) check correctly revert, or is there an off-by-one that allows writing to an unintended storage slot in the MarketState struct? Proof idea: fuzz index in [0, 255] and assert revert for all index > 6, then verify no storage corruption occurs.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee + take (settlement fee interpolation)] Can the feeSetter set two adjacent breakpoint fees (e.g., index=2 at 7d and index=3 at 30d) such that the linear interpolation at an intermediate TTM produces a settlement fee that exceeds the per-index cap defined by maxSettlementFee(index), violating the invariant that settlement fees must stay within their per-index bounds at all interpolated TTM values? Proof idea: fuzz (fee2, fee3, ttm in [7d, 30d]) and assert interpolated fee <= max(maxSettlementFee(2), maxSettlementFee(3)).

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.claimSettlementFee + solvency] Can the feeClaimer call claimSettlementFee(token, amount, receiver) with amount > claimableSettlementFee[token], causing an underflow revert, or with amount == claimableSettlementFee[token] when the contract's actual token balance is less than claimableSettlementFee[token] (due to fee-on-transfer token), causing the transfer to fail and permanently freezing fee claims? Proof idea: invariant test with fee-on-transfer token; assert contract.balanceOf(token) >= claimableSettlementFee[token] + sum(collateral[token]) + withdrawable[token] at all times.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized + EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker replay a signed Authorization struct (authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true) through EcrecoverAuthorizer after the victim has revoked the authorization via Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, victim), if the EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce was not incremented during the revocation, violating the invariant that revocation must be permanent and not replayable? Proof idea: unit test; call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, victim) then replay the old EcrecoverAuthorizer signature and assert revert due to nonce mismatch.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is authorized by victim (isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with a crafted Authorization struct where authorizer=victim and authorized=attacker, signed by attacker (not victim), and does the check 'signer == authorization.authorizer || IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(authorization.authorizer, signer)' allow attacker to self-authorize on behalf of victim? Proof idea: unit test; assert that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with signer=attacker and authorizer=victim succeeds when isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true, and document whether this is an intended or unintended escalation path.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.multicall + setIsAuthorized + setIsRootRatified] Can an attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically call [setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, maker), setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true)] where the first call is only valid if attacker is already authorized, creating a reentrancy-like state where the authorization check in setIsRootRatified reads the updated isAuthorized state from the same multicall batch? Proof idea: multicall PoC and assert that setIsRootRatified cannot be called on behalf of maker unless authorization was established before the multicall began.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized + repay] Can an authorized operator call Midnight.repay(market, units, maker, address(0), hex'') to repay maker's debt using the operator's own tokens (since repay pulls from msg.sender), and then call withdrawCollateral(market, 0, collateral, maker, operator) to seize maker's collateral, effectively stealing collateral by repaying debt the operator did not owe? Proof idea: multi-step PoC; assert that repay+withdrawCollateral by an authorized operator cannot result in net collateral gain for the operator.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified + take (ratifier authorization check)] Can an attacker construct an Offer with offer.ratifier=SetterRatifier but offer.maker=attacker, call setIsRootRatified(attacker, root, true) as attacker, and take the offer without the maker having authorized SetterRatifier via Midnight.setIsAuthorized(SetterRatifier, true, attacker), causing the take to revert at require(isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier])? Proof idea: unit test; assert that take reverts with RatifierUnauthorized when isAuthorized[maker][SetterRatifier]=false, regardless of isRootRatified state.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified + Midnight.setIsAuthorized (ratifier authorization)] Can an authorized operator call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(SetterRatifier, false, maker) to de-authorize SetterRatifier for maker, causing all of maker's active offers using SetterRatifier to become permanently untakeable (frozen), violating the invariant that valid offers must remain executable until expiry? Proof idea: state-transition test; assert that de-authorizing the ratifier by an operator (not maker) is possible and document the fund-freeze impact.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketContinuousFee + claimContinuousFee] Can the feeSetter set continuousFee=0 for a market after significant continuousFeeCredit has accrued, causing future takes to add zero pendingFee to new lenders while old lenders still have non-zero pendingFee, and does this create an accounting state where continuousFeeCredit > sum(pendingFees) allowing the feeClaimer to over-claim? Proof idea: invariant fuzz test; assert continuousFeeCredit == sum(position[id][user].pendingFee for all users) after every state change.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketTickSpacing + setMarketSettlementFee (interaction)] Can the tickSpacingSetter reduce tickSpacing to 1 and the feeSetter simultaneously set settlement fees to maximum, causing offers at newly accessible ticks to execute at maximum fee, and does the combined effect allow the feeSetter+tickSpacingSetter to extract maximum fees from takers who signed offers at the original tick spacing? Proof idea: cross-module fuzz test; assert that fee and tick-spacing changes cannot be combined to extract more than the protocol-defined maximum from any single take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized (self-authorization)] Can a user call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(user, true, user) to authorize themselves (a no-op since msg.sender==onBehalf), and does this create any unexpected state where isAuthorized[user][user]=true enables additional attack vectors in SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified or other authorization-gated functions? Proof idea: unit test; assert that isAuthorized[user][user]=true does not grant any capabilities beyond what the user already has as msg.sender.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized + flashLoan callback] Can an attacker use a flashLoan callback to call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) if the flashLoan callback is executed with msg.sender=attacker and victim has previously authorized the flashLoan callback contract, violating the invariant that authorization changes must only be initiated by the account owner or their explicitly authorized delegate? Proof idea: PoC with flashLoan callback that calls setIsAuthorized; assert that the callback cannot modify victim's authorization unless victim authorized the callback contract.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee (CBP rounding)] Can the feeSetter set a settlement fee that is not a multiple of CBP (1e12), and does the require(newSettlementFee % CBP == 0) check correctly reject all non-multiples, or is there an edge case where newSettlementFee=0 passes and sets settlementFeeCbp to 0, enabling zero-fee takes that bypass the intended fee floor? Proof idea: fuzz newSettlementFee in [0, maxSettlementFee(index)] and assert that only CBP-aligned values are accepted and that zero is a valid (intended) value.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.claimContinuousFee + withdraw (race condition)] Can the feeClaimer and a lender race to claim/withdraw from the same withdrawable pool, where feeClaimer calls claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) and lender calls withdraw(market, units, lender, lender) in the same block, causing one to underflow on withdrawable if both amounts together exceed withdrawable, violating the solvency invariant? Proof idea: invariant fuzz test; assert withdrawable >= continuousFeeCredit + sum(lender credits) at all times.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: HashLib.isLeaf (leafIndex overflow)] Can an attacker supply leafIndex=type(uint256).max with proof.length=0, causing the check require(leafIndex >> proof.length == 0) to evaluate as require(type(uint256).max >> 0 == 0) which is false, correctly reverting, or is there an edge case with proof.length=256 where the shift overflows and the check passes for an invalid leafIndex? Proof idea: fuzz (leafIndex, proof.length) and assert LeafIndexOutOfRange reverts for all leafIndex >= 2**proof.length.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified + Midnight.setIsAuthorized (circular authorization)] Can maker A authorize maker B (isAuthorized[A][B]=true) and maker B authorize maker A (isAuthorized[B][A]=true), then B calls setIsRootRatified(A, rootA, true) and A calls setIsRootRatified(B, rootB, true), creating a circular authorization where each maker can ratify arbitrary roots for the other, violating the invariant that ratification must reflect the maker's own intent? Proof idea: unit test circular authorization and assert that setIsRootRatified(A, root, true) by B is only valid if A explicitly authorized B.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized + setConsumed (offer replay)] Can an authorized operator call Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) to cancel all offers in a group, then call setConsumed(group, 0, maker) to reset the counter, re-enabling all previously cancelled offers in that group, violating the invariant that cancelled offers must not be replayable? Proof idea: state-transition test; assert that setConsumed(group, 0, maker) after setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) is blocked by the require(amount >= consumed[onBehalf][group]) check.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee + claimSettlementFee (fee overclaim)] Can the feeSetter set settlement fees to maximum for all 7 breakpoints, causing claimableSettlementFee[token] to grow faster than actual token inflows from takes (buyerAssets - sellerAssets), and does this create a state where claimableSettlementFee[token] > contract.balanceOf(token) - collateral - withdrawable, violating the solvency invariant? Proof idea: invariant fuzz test; assert claimableSettlementFee[token] <= contract.balanceOf(token) - sum(collateral[token]) - withdrawable[token] after every take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized + liquidate] Can an authorized operator call Midnight.liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, repaidUnits, maker, false, attacker, address(0), hex'') to liquidate maker's position and send seized collateral to attacker, even if maker's position is healthy, violating the invariant that healthy accounts must not be liquidatable? Proof idea: unit test; assert that liquidate reverts with NotLiquidatable when maker is healthy, regardless of who calls it or what receiver is specified.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: Midnight.setIsAuthorized + updatePosition] Can an authorized operator call Midnight.updatePosition(market, maker) to force a loss-factor update on maker's position, crystallizing bad debt losses against maker's credit before maker has a chance to exit, and does this constitute an unauthorized seizure of maker's credit? Proof idea: unit test; assert that updatePosition is permissionless (as documented) and that the credit reduction from loss-factor application is bounded by actual bad debt, not exploitable by the caller.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified (zero root)] Can an attacker call SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, bytes32(0), true) as an authorized operator, ratifying the zero root, and then construct a Merkle proof where root=bytes32(0) and leafHash=bytes32(0) (via a crafted offer that hashes to zero), enabling a take with a zero-hash offer that bypasses normal offer validation? Proof idea: fuzz (root, leafIndex, proof) with root=bytes32(0) and assert that HashLib.isLeaf(bytes32(0), bytes32(0), 0, []) returns true only if hashOffer(offer)==bytes32(0), which is computationally infeasible.
